dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain at Chimborazo Place, [CITY].
Hi, I'm Daniel, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I'm experiencing chest tightness and some blurry vision.
Can you look at me and tell me your name, please?
Patrice, 63 years old.
And where are you when you called 911?
I was at my apartment, I leaned on the couch, and suddenly had chest tightness and some blurry vision.
Have you had similar issues before?
No. I have a history of asthma and anxiety, but not chest tightness.
What medications do you take regularly?
Amlodipine, lisinopril, carvedilol, metformin, glypizide, and albuterol as needed for asthma.
Any allergies we should know about?
I'm allergic to penicillin-nothing else I'm aware of.
Can you speak clearly? Do you have any trouble with your throat or breathing?
No, my airway seems fine. I can speak and breathe.
How would you describe your breathing now?
It's about the same as usual, maybe a bit faster than usual, but nothing alarming-no wheezing now.
Ma'am, we'll be taking some readings now. How about we start with an oximeter for your oxygen and heart rate?
Your oxygen saturation is 99%-good.
Your breathing rate is 20-normal depth and not labored.
Your pulse is 99 and strong-peripheral pulses also feel good.
And your blood pressure is 171 over 132. We'll check that again in a few minutes.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how severe is the chest tightness right now?
It feels tight and pressing-like something is pressing down on my chest-not sharp or burning. I would say about a 4 now.
Does this tightness stay in your chest or radiate to any other areas?
It stays in the middle of my chest. No movement elsewhere. I did have blurry vision earlier, but it passed.
May we check your blood sugar now with a finger stick? It will be a quick pinch.
Sure, go ahead.
Your blood sugar is currently at 101 mg/dL-not low. We had a previous reading lower about an hour ago, so we'll keep checking.
We will now apply a four-lead monitor to watch your heart.
Okay. What does it show?
It monitors your heart rhythm and rate so we can watch for changes.
Your skin looks clear, warm, and dry. No rashes, peeling, or other changes.
We'll now do a quick look to check for anything obvious in your airway. Nothing abnormal detected.
Would you be okay with me listening to your lungs now?
Sure, ahead with that.
You have clear breath sounds on both sides, nothing in the way of wheezing or other unusual sounds. Chest movements seem even and symmetrical.
Eyes are clear, pupils are equally reactive, no signs of swelling or rigid neck. You're alert and clear-no stroke concerns noted so far.
Based on vitals, ECG readings, and exam, we'll follow the chest pain protocol-hold off transport until treatments complete.
We're considering aspirin for chest discomfort. You okay with that? Any known aspirin allergy or serious GI history?
No aspirin allergy. I haven't had GI bleeding issues.
Then we'll administer 324 mg of aspirin now-just like protocol.
We also need to start an IV in case we need it later during transport.
IV established successfully in your right arm. Held securely in place.
Your heart rate has dropped to 95-still within normal range.
Breathing rate remains 20-normal and consistent.
Blood pressure is now 186 over 83. Systolic is up slightly, but we're monitoring that.
Let me check your blood sugar again-just to confirm and ensure no machine issues.
Oxygen saturation is still steady at 99%.
We'll call ahead to [COMPANY_NAME]-need to give them a bit of your background, your IV access, and aspirin administration.
You look steady enough. Can we move you to the stretcher now?
You mentioned before an episode where you thought you were wheezing-can you describe it briefly before movement?
It felt overwhelming. I thought I was choking, intense and scary-almost like a panic episode.
Securing you with five-point restraints now for a safe and stable ride.
You'll be taken directly to ED bed 25 at [COMPANY_NAME]. No need for triage-awaiting your arrival there.
Once we get you to the ER, you'll go straight to bed 25. You can walk there directly if you prefer, as you requested earlier.
That's fine. Appreciate the minimal effort.
Patient is alert, vitals stable, IV established, aspirin administered per protocol-awaiting ED bed 25.
Patient has GCS 15, stable, following all protocol, awaiting ED transfer.
Patient continues to be independent with steady gait-consistent throughout assessment and transport.
